children.name: Paradisaea
children.description: The classic birds of paradise, with elongated flank plumes and iridescent plumage. Males perform elaborate courtship displays at communal leks, and the genus gave the entire family its common name after early explorers marvelled at preserved skins brought back from New Guinea.

children.name: Paradisaea apoda
children.description: The species that first introduced Europeans to the family when early traders shipped plucked skins to Europe, leading Linnaeus to name it 'apoda' (footless). Males perform inverted pendulum displays at leks, suspending themselves upside down with shimmering plumes fully fanned.

children.name: Paradisaea raggiana
children.description: Papua New Guinea's national bird, famed for its spectacular crimson flank plumes. Males gather in treetop leks where they call raucously then erupt into synchronised wing-spreading dances, each competing to impress the perching females above.

children.name: Paradisaea rudolphi
children.description: The only Paradisaea with a predominantly black-and-blue plumage, lacking the typical red flank plumes. Males perform an unusual inverted display from a horizontal perch, swaying back and forth with wings extended.

children.name: Cicinnurus
children.description: A genus of stunning small birds of paradise noted for brilliant colours and uniquely twisted central tail wires that end in ornamental spirals. Males perform intricate micro-lek displays on fallen logs, showcasing their iridescent gorgets and fanned tail feathers.

children.name: Cicinnurus respublica
children.description: A dazzling miniature gem of the Raja Ampat islands, with a bare blue crown crossed by two black bars and a vivid crimson back. The male's tail wires curl into perfect emerald spirals that he displays on a cleared patch of forest floor.

children.name: Cicinnurus regius
children.description: The smallest and most vividly coloured bird of paradise, brilliant scarlet above with white underparts and two long tail wires ending in emerald discs. Males perform a curious bouncing dance on a vertical sapling, fanning their throat feathers into a metallic green shield.

children.name: Cicinnurus magnificus
children.description: Formerly placed in its own genus Diphyllodes, this species has a stunning yellow mantle and iridescent belly. Males clear a display arena and hang upside down from a vine, slowly opening their wings to reveal a vivid blue-green breast shield.

children.name: Lophorina
children.description: A genus best known for the superb bird of paradise's iconic 'smiley face' courtship display, achieved by raising a glossy blue-black breast shield into a perfectly circular, iridescent frill. Recent molecular work has expanded the genus to include several former Ptiloris species.

children.name: Lophorina superba
children.description: Famous for its remarkable courtship display in which the male erects a highly iridescent blue breast shield and cape to form a black oval with two bright blue crescents, resembling a broad cartoonish face. The female, drab brown, judges the male's frantic hopping from a nearby perch.

children.name: Seleucidis
children.description: A monotypic genus with the twelve-wired bird of paradise, named for the twelve stiff, wire-like shaft feathers that emerge from the flank plumes. The male's display is unique among the family, consisting of a static pose held on a vertical perch.

children.name: Seleucidis melanoleucus
children.description: A mostly black bird of paradise with a prominent yellow flank plume and twelve wire-like feathers that curve upward from the body. Unlike other birds of paradise, the male displays in silence, holding a rigid posture on a vertical perch while the female inspects him closely.

children.name: Semioptera
children.description: A monotypic genus endemic to the Maluku Islands of Indonesia, where Wallace's standardwing bird of paradise was first described by Alfred Russel Wallace. Unique among birds of paradise for having elongated white wing epaulettes rather than flank plumes.

children.name: Semioptera wallacii
children.description: Discovered by Alfred Russel Wallace on Halmahera in 1858, the male has two pairs of long white wing epaulettes that flutter like flags during an aerial display. Males gather in high canopy display trees where they leap upward, calling with nasal squeaks.
